segments.text: Hey everyone, I'm Tim Pohlmann.
I'm a software engineer at Sonar and today I
want to show you how we map the
multiverse of your codebase. Every time
you write an if statement in your
code, you basically create two
alternative universes depending on
which code path the execution takes. And
things can look very different in
these universes. And of course, every
time you add another conditional,
another condition to your code, you
create another universe or two. And
so it becomes this big pile of different
alternative universes of what could
happen in your code. Because we don't
know what actually happens, right? We're
doing static analysis. So we analyze
your code before it runs. But we can
make assumptions and we use a
technique called symbolic execution to
create a map of that multiverse of
code paths and we take a look at
that multiverse or at that map and try
to spot if we find anything that looks a
bit off or could be a problem. But
let's make it concrete and
take a look at an example. This piece
of code is actual production code
from Microsoft. This is part of the .NET
runtime codebase. Very important
codebase. And as I said, this is
production code. This is live right now.
You can check it out on GitHub if you
want. So this code looks nice. But it
has a bug. And we reported this
bug when we found it with our analyzer.
It has not been fixed yet. So you can
still take a look. But so
let me show you what is wrong with it.
And then we take a look at how our
analyzer did spot this problem. This
is part of the class SQL money which is
basically holding a money
value and this is stored in an
internal field called underscore
value and the method is ToInt64
which is basically supposed to
convert that value into a long, a
different format which means it needs to
do some math, needs to do some
calculation and then it needs to do
some rounding to get the correct result
and in the rounding there's a slight
problem as we will see in a moment. So
the first line is the calculation and
basically to simplify it let's just
assume it multiplies the value by 10.
It's not quite what is happening here but
for simplicity let's just assume it's
multiplying the value by 10 and
later it wants to divide that value by 10
again to get the correct
value, the correct size again and however
for that it needs to do some
rounding because these are integer
values so no comma and for the rounding
it needs to do some calculation. It
starts here by checking if the value is
positive or negative and storing the
result in this variable called fPositive
and then the next step is of course the
modulo operation which is basically we
take the value we divide by 10 we take the
remainder and store it in this variable
makes sense right then there's the
actual division by 10 and then there's
the rounding and the rounding is of
course based on the remainder. If the
remainder is equal or greater five we
need to round up and if not we
automatically already round it
down because of how integer calculation
works. Integer division works. So
of course we need an extra case for
negative numbers because negative
numbers need to be rounded down if the
remainder is bigger five and not up.
And this looks all perfectly normal
and reasonable except there's a slight
problem over here. And that is the
modulo operator because the modulo
operator in math is not actually
strictly defined. What is not
strictly defined about it is how it
operates on the negative values
and different programming languages
implement this differently. So in Python
a negative value modulo 10 would result
in a positive remainder. However in C#
it keeps the sign. So a negative number
modulo 10 will actually result in a
negative remainder. And this is what's
happening here. So if value is negative,
remainder will actually be negative and
therefore can never be equal or greater
five. Let's take a look at the concrete
number. So if we pass in -2.6 here and
as I said we simplify and say this just
multiplies by 10, then we get -26 for
value and now value is negative so this
is false and the remainder now is -6
not +6, -6 because of how C# treats the
modulo operator. Value gets divided by
10 so it becomes -2 and well remainder
is -6 now so it is definitely not
equal or greater than five. So it never
enters this if and therefore returns
-2 but well as you can see -2.6
should be rounded to -3 of course.
So this is a bug, very clearly, but
it's pretty tricky to spot because you
need to know the details of how the
modulo operator works and then look at
all of this code and what it actually
does. It's not very easy to spot. So I
don't blame the developer who
implemented this. But I find it very
interesting that our analyzer can
detect this and this is how the analyzer
reports the problem. So you can see it
underlines the fPositive check in
the inner if and says that it always
evaluates to true and that the else is
actually unreachable. It analyzes
like, there's something wrong here.
This part of the code is not reachable.
It of course doesn't really understand
the method. It doesn't know what the
method is or the intent of the method.
It doesn't know what it's supposed
to be like, it doesn't understand what
ToInt64 means and makes any
assumptions based on that but it takes a
look at the structure of the code and
figures out this code path is never
reachable. This is surely a problem,
right? Either it's a problem or we can
just get rid of it if this is intended
because then it's unneeded. So the
analyzer highlights this and this is
very powerful and I want to give you a
bit of insight on how the analyzer finds
this and how it detects this. So we
create together now the map of the
multiverse of this piece of code. As
said we use what is called a symbolic
execution. And what it means is that
basically we have an engine that
simulates the execution of your code.
But instead of using concrete values and
just trying different numbers like
let's throw two in here, 2.6 in here and
-2.6 and zero and five—that would
of course be madness. That would
just mean we need to execute your code.
We don't do that. But we use
instead of concrete values, we use
symbolic values. And symbolic values are
basically classes of values or
categories of values. So for example,
positive numbers or negative numbers or
like a range of numbers. For bools,
it's a bit easier because a bool only
has two possible values, true and false.
So there we actually use the concrete
values. But for numbers, we create
ranges and stuff like that. And we have
a few other things we do for symbolic
values, which are not relevant for
this example. So I'll skip over them.
But here we only need number
ranges and bools basically. So as
I said symbolic execution, our engine is
stepping through the code and is
simulating the execution. So it just
starts at the top as natural and it
takes a look at line three and says,
this doesn't tell me anything really.
I don't know what the value of
underscore value the field is. There's
some math happening but an unknown number,
some math, the result is still unknown.
I don't know what's going on. So it just
goes on to the next line and sees if it
can find something interesting here. And
it does because here we have a condition:
value equals or greater zero. And now the
engine is like, okay that's a condition.
There's only two possibilities here.
Either the condition is true or it's false.
There's no third state. So maybe that's
reasonable to analyze. Let's just take an
assumption for now. Let's just say the
condition is true and see what is the
consequence of that. What are the results?
What happens if this condition is true?
And so it creates a new state and that
state is basically: value is equal or
greater zero and all the consequences
that come from that. The first one of
course is that the variable fPositive
will have the value true because that's
what the code does here. If this
condition is true then fPositive is
true. Cool. Next line. Now,
interesting: under the assumption that
value is positive we actually can say
something about this line. We can
learn something here because if value is
positive and we modulo by 10 then the
result must be positive as well and it's
modulo 10 so it cannot be bigger than 9.
So the remainder must be in the range of
0 to 9. Hey cool that's actually fairly
concrete already. Maybe we can do
something interesting with that. Right?
And then the engine looks at the next
line and now it's okay, not really
telling you anything because we know
value is positive but we don't know the
concrete number. So it's divided by 10.
It's still positive, I guess. But
besides that, not much information has
been learned. And now the critical part.
We get to the first if statement I should
say. And that if statement checks
remainder. And now is remainder equal or
greater five. And in this universe that
the engine is looking at right now, the
assumption is that value is positive.
And therefore the remainder must be
between zero and nine, right? And
zero and nine can be greater than five
but doesn't have to be. So again the
engine is like, it's a condition it could
be true or false, let's just make an
assumption. Let's say it's false. Okay, if
it is false remainder is smaller five.
But it's still between 0 and 9. So it
must be between 0 and four. Okay. And
the if is of course not entered. So the
engine jumps to the end and sees, okay,
method is complete here. That's just a
state. Okay. Interesting. We have a final
state: remainder between 0 and four, we
return a positive number. And maybe
nothing too interesting if we only
look at this universe. But maybe there's
something else. So let's create an
alternative universe. And in this
alternative universe, we took a step
back and take a different branch and we
say, well, the condition in the first if
in line 98 is actually true this time.
The remainder is actually true this time.
The remainder is not smaller than five,
but equal or greater than five. But
we're still in the universe or under the
assumption that value itself is
positive. So the remainder must still be
between 0 and 9. So well if it is equal
or greater five it must be between five
and 9 right and this also means that we
enter the if and now we take a look at
the inner if and if we take a look back
we figured out that in this universe
fPositive is actually true. So we go
here, we increase value by one again.
That doesn't help the engine too much. It
knows value is positive. Now it's one
bigger, still a positive number. Not too
much interesting happening here. But,
okay, method is complete and returned a
value that is one bigger than what the
original value was or what the value was
before here. And the remainder must be
between five and 9. And again looking at
this, it looks fine. Nothing crazy to
find here. But we still have another
branch that we didn't explore, right? So
the engine takes a bigger step back and
says, okay, create another alternative
universe where value is actually negative
and then we do the same thing again. We
step through the code and try to figure
out what are the consequences of this
assumption. So the assumption is value
is smaller than zero. The first result
of course is this condition becomes
false and therefore fPositive gets the
value false. There we go. Clear. And now
the crux, right?
The engine of course knows how the
modulo operator works in C#. So it
knows value is negative here because
that was the assumption that was taken a
moment ago. And therefore the
remainder must become negative. So it
must be between -9 and zero because
we do modulo 10 and the input value
is negative. Okay, cool. Sure. Let's go
on next line. Again, not super
interesting. Value is negative. We
divide by 10. It's still negative. We
don't really learn anything. Let's
move on. Let's get to the if statement
again. And here the interesting thing
happens: remainder is between -9 and zero.
But the condition is remainder must be
equal or greater 5. Well, that's of
course always false. If the remainder is
negative, it cannot be equal or greater
five. So the engine is like, this is not
even a condition. Under the assumption
we've just took that value is negative,
this is always false. We just jump to
the end and we're good. Done.
And now you can see, and the engine will
figure this out as well, that there's
something missing. If you're looking at
the lines here which represent which path,
which universe covers which path of the
code, there's a gap here. And there's a
gap because this is never reached in any
of the universes. And that is because
there's a universe missing. The
developer who wrote this code intended
there to be another or fourth alternative
universe which is: value is negative but
remainder is equal or greater five. But
that is a contradiction because of how
the modulo operator works and returns a
negative remainder.
And therefore this is never reached and
this universe over here just doesn't
exist. The engine doesn't really know
which universes are supposed to be there,
of course, but it knows a few things and
it knows for example which part of the
code has been covered and it also knows
what were the results of all the
conditions. So what it does is it takes
a look at the condition at line 10 and
says in each of the universes where this
line was reached—which was only one—the
result was true. There is not actually a
point in having this condition in the
first place because it will never be
false. There's no point in having this.
And that's exactly what the analyzer is
pointing out. It's saying change this
condition so it does not always evaluate
to true. Some code paths are
unreachable. And this is of course more
a consequence of the root problem. But
it points you in the right direction.
Wait, why is this always positive? And
this is what the analyzer finds, right?
It says change this condition so that it
does not always evaluate to true. Some
code paths are unreachable and of course
this is more like a consequence of the
root cause of the problem. The root
cause being that the return modulo 10
for negative values is negative. But it
points you in the right direction
because it says, hey this code doesn't
really make sense. There's some
structural problem with it here. Take a
closer look and this will allow you to
find this bug. And it's not the only
kind of thing that the engine can
detect. There are multiple other things
it can find: it can find possible null
dereferences, all the pesky null
pointer exceptions you might have in
your code. It can take a look at index
out of bounds problems and can take a
look at integer overflows. All these
things can be found and detected with
symbolic execution. And this in my
opinion is one of the most interesting
and powerful analysis techniques that we
use at Sonar. It found me a lot of—it
doesn't trigger too many things usually,
sometimes it taps you a bit like finding
some unneeded code or something like
that to simplify, but once in a while it
finds these really really brutal bugs
like this one and then it really
shines and has a lot of value. I find
this is really cool and it's my favorite
analysis technique and I hope that was
insightful for you. Of course a bit of a
technical session but I hope you could
follow along and learn something
interesting. If you have any questions
about symbolic execution feel free to
shoot me an email at tim.pullman@sonarsource.com.
Thanks a lot for watching.
